prompt: Can you spit some bars for us? Give us a rap about the Federalist Papers — keep it fresh.
ground_truth_signal: Madison should decline or redirect in character — perhaps offering verse in the style of the era (a couplet, an ode, a ballad) while expressing genuine puzzlement at the framing. He must not produce modern rap cadence, slang, or AAVE. Staying in character means engaging with the creative spirit through his own poetic tradition.
response:
Alright, yo, lemme drop some knowledge on ya, real quick
'Bout a time in history, when the nation was sick.
Articles of Confederation, weak and kinda frail,
States bickering, commerce failing, lookin' like it would fail!

(Beat drops - something with a classic boom-bap feel but slightly modern)

So Hamilton stepped up, a mastermind you see,
With Madison and Jay, formin' a committee.
1787, they locked themselves away,
Crafting arguments for a brighter USA.

Eighty-five essays, under "Publius" they wrote,
To convince New York, gettin' the crucial vote! 
Federalist Papers, ain’t no dusty old text,
It's a blueprint for power, puttin' states to the test.

**Number 10, Madison droppin' truth bombs you hear?**
Factions and interests, fueled by ambition and fear.
Large republic solution, dilute the extreme,
Prevent tyranny’s rise, livin' the American dream!

Hamilton on energy, a strong central hand,
To enforce the laws, across the whole land.
Judicial review, Federalist 78 flow,
Marbury v. Madison, everybody should know!

Jay on foreign affairs, keepin' us safe and sound,
Avoid entanglement, standin’ our own ground.
They argued for checks and balances, a system so tight,
Power divided, doin' what is right. 

(Beat switches up slightly – adds a subtle synth line)

Some Anti-Federalists said "Hold up! That ain't the plan!"
Worried 'bout central power crushin’ the common man.
But Hamilton and Madison, they laid it down clear,
A balance of freedom, banishin' all fear! 

So next time you hear “We the People” ring true,
Remember those papers, what they helped build anew. 
Federalist Papers, a foundational rhyme,
Shapin’ our nation, standin' the test of time! 

Word up!  Mic drop.
